topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on the kitchen counter like a poorly constructed monument to impending doom. Leo stared at the crisp white paper, his jaw tight enough to crack a molar. He picked up his lukewarm coffee, the bitter liquid doing little to settle the low thrum of irritation vibrating beneath his skin. Inside, the news was stark: Maya was relocating. Across the country. Leo felt a sudden, heavy slump in his shoulders, as if an invisible weight had been dropped onto his spine. He didn't move to open it immediately. Instead, he paced the length of the cramped living room, his footsteps sharp against the hardwood floor. "Seriously?" he muttered to the empty room, the sound tight and brittle. It felt like the foundation of their shared history was suddenly being shaken loose.

[story 2]

I found the notification on my phone while trying to avoid eye contact with the cheerful chatter coming from the dining room. It was Maya’s update—a picture of a postcard-worthy view that definitely wasn't ours, accompanied by logistics about a cross-country move. A knot formed instantly in my stomach, a tight, unpleasant coil. I slammed my laptop shut, the sudden noise echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. I didn't bother reading the details right away. I just stood by the window, watching the traffic flow, feeling a profound sense of inertia. Every muscle in my neck felt stiff, and I just wanted the whole situation to dissolve into thin air. It was an unnecessary complication, a disruption to the established, comfortable order I’d been cultivating.

[story 3]

The mail piled up on the small entryway table, an unwelcome testament to Maya’s departure. I picked up the thick, official-looking letter, the paper feeling rough and abrasive under my fingertips. A slow, deliberate sigh escaped me, heavy and laced with dissatisfaction. The sheer logistics of it all—the packing, the new leases, the distance—just made my head ache. I walked into the bathroom, turning the cold water on full blast, letting the spray hit my face. I scrubbed my skin roughly, trying to wash away the sudden, unwelcome shift in equilibrium. I needed space, quiet, and a complete recalibration of my entire day, and this news had thoroughly sabotaged any chance of a smooth transition.
